##section:executive_summary
The archaeological evaluation of the Mock Evaluation project (Project ID: mock_evaluation) involved a systematic excavation of a potential boundary ditch (context [102]) and its associated fills and deposits. Excavation was carried out in a controlled manner, with recording of stratigraphy, sedimentological and finds data, and environmental sampling. Key findings include a Roman-period primary fill deposit ([104]) and a secondary fill ([103]) within a ditch that also cuts a Late Pleistocene layer ([105]) and a modern topsoil horizon ([101]). The excavation recovered 44 finds across eight entries, with significant pottery and animal bone material from the Roman layers, and a range of inclusions including flint, charcoal, and fired clay. 

##section:introduction
The site is situated in the historic_england_cl3 jurisdiction, within an area of historic interest that warrants further study. The project background involves a formal evaluation of a possible Romano-British and Roman settlement or agricultural site. The NGR coordinates are 200.00 m East, 500.00 m North (to be updated with full NGR data). The local geology consists of a sedimentary sequence of clayey silt and silty loam, with a paleosol horizon that shows evidence of human activity. Topography is gently undulating, with a slight north‑south gradient that influences the orientation of the identified ditch. 

##section:methodology
Excavation was undertaken in a 1 m grid, with a focus on the preservation of stratigraphic integrity and the documentation of all contexts. The recording system employed a Harris Matrix approach, with each context labelled by its type, period, and descriptive notes. Finds were collected in numbered bags and recorded with context, sample, and find numbers. Sampling policy involved bulk soil sampling for flotation in contexts [103] and [104], yielding two samples (SAMP001 and SAMP002). Photo documentation was carried out in context [102] (image DSC_0042.jpg), providing a section face that shows the cut and its fills. 

##section:stratigraphic_narrative
The principal feature of the excavation is a linear cut (context [102]) aligned N‑S, with steep concave sides and a flattish base. The cut measures 1.2 m wide and 0.45 m deep, and it cuts through a primary fill deposit (context [104]), a secondary fill deposit (context [103]), and a Late Pleistocene layer (context [105]). 

The primary fill deposit ([104]) is a dark greyish‑black silty clay that contains frequent charcoal inclusions (5–15 %) and moderate sub‑angular flint. Occasional fired‑clay fragments are present, suggesting an oven lining. This deposit represents the initial silting after the ditch was excavated, and it is filled by the secondary fill deposit ([103]).

The secondary fill deposit ([103]) is a mid‑brown clayey silt that contains occasional small sub‑rounded gravel and occasional charcoal fragments. The compaction is firm, and the sediment is moderately sorted. Inclusions include sub‑angular flint and occasional small flint gravel, with a moderate frequency of sub‑angular flint (5–20 mm) and occasional charcoal flecks. This deposit is the secondary fill of the ditch and is followed by a modern topsoil horizon ([101]).

The modern topsoil horizon ([101]) presents a dark greyish‑brown silty loam that contains moderate frequency sub‑angular flint inclusions (5–20 mm) and occasional charcoal flecks. This layer is interpreted as a ploughsoil horizon and represents the modern ground surface prior to the present day. It is filled by the cut [102], indicating that the ditch was re‑filled and the site was used as a boundary ditch or drainage channel, possibly in the Romano‑British period.

The Late Pleistocene layer ([105]) is a mid‑yellowish‑brown clayey silt that contains frequent sub‑angular and sub‑rounded chalk fragments (10–40 mm) and rare small flint gravel. This layer was cut by the ditch [102] and represents a weathered natural or head deposit, likely deposited during the Late Pleistocene periglacial period.

##section:finds_summary
A total of 44 finds were recovered across eight entries. The most significant concentrations are in the Roman deposits: 12 pottery items (greyware sherds and a Samian ware base sherd) and 8 animal‑bone fragments (cattle‑sized long‑bone fragments) in context [103]; 5 pottery items (grey‑ware body sherds) and 3 animal‑bone fragments (burnt bone fragments) in context [104]; and 6 fired‑clay fragments (amorphous fragments, possible oven lining) also in context [104]. Post‑medieval pottery (3 items, glazed red earthenware), post‑medieval CBM (5 items, fragmentary roof tile), and modern glass (2 items, green bottle glass) were recovered from context [101]. 

##section:environmental_summary
Two bulk‑soil samples were collected for flotation. Sample SAMP001 from context [103] yielded 40 L of bulk soil, and sample SAMP002 from context [104] yielded 30 L of charcoal‑rich deposit. Flotation results indicated a good yield of charcoal and inclusions, supporting the interpretation of the primary fill as a Roman-era deposit. 

##section:discussion
The presence of a Roman-period primary fill and a secondary fill within a boundary ditch suggests continuous use of the site from the Romano‑British period through to the Roman period. The recovery of fired‑clay fragments may indicate in‑situ activity such as an oven or hearth. The modern topsoil horizon shows evidence of post‑medieval activity, and the presence of glass and CBM suggests later use. The Late Pleistocene layer indicates that the site has a long history of sediment accumulation. In regional context, this site may represent a small settlement or agricultural outpost. 

##section:archive_statement
It is recommended that the excavation data, including context records, finds, samples, and photographic documentation, be deposited in the local archive as part of the mock_evaluation project. Accession number 2026‑05‑24 should be used for record‑keeping. All data should be stored in a digital format and made available to future research.
